petitioner_background: The petitioner is a scientist in the field of chemistry who earned his Ph.D. and at the time of filing was working as a Visiting Research Scientist and Executive Director. He sought classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences. His research focused on nanoporous silicas, nanoparticulate materials, zeolite encapsulated metal catalysis, and solid acidic catalysts. The petition was filed on July 22, 2002.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Correspondence from the Associate Editor of the Journal of the American Chemical Society and the Senior Editor of The Journal of Physical Chemistry reflecting that the petitioner was requested to review an aggregate total of three manuscripts. On appeal, the petitioner submitted two e-mails from September and October 2002 reflecting that he reviewed an additional manuscript for The Journal of Physical Chemistry. The appellate submission also included an undated letter from a Program Officer requesting evaluation of a research proposal, addressed 'Dear Colleague' and not specifically identifying the petitioner.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the September/October 2002 e-mails came into existence subsequent to the petition's filing date and could not retroactively establish eligibility, citing 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. The 'Dear Colleague' letter did not specifically identify the petitioner and the record contained no evidence showing the petitioner completed this research proposal evaluation as of the filing date. Regarding the three manuscripts reviewed as of the filing date, the AAO noted that peer review of manuscripts is a routine element of the process by which articles are selected for publication in scholarly journals, and occasional participation in peer review does not automatically demonstrate sustained national or international acclaim at the very top of the field. Without evidence setting the petitioner apart from others in his field—such as peer-reviewing an unusually large number of manuscripts, receiving multiple independent requests from a substantial number of journals, or serving in an editorial position for a distinguished journal—the AAO concluded the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Initial letters of support from individuals from the petitioner's affiliated institution. On appeal, the petitioner submitted letters of support from several prominent witnesses outside his circle of acquaintances, including statements that the petitioner distinguished himself in the synthesis of very high surface area nanoporous silicas reaching 1700m² per gram (the highest surface area the witness was aware of), and that he was credited with being the first to demonstrate silica hybrid structure of porous and lamellar—with applications in drug delivery materials, hydrogen storage, and additives for personal care and cleaning products. Another letter from a writer with no personal or professional relationship discussed the petitioner's work on zeolite encapsulated metal catalysis and solid acidic catalysts, forming the basis for new products in chemical and petroleum industries, and his work with high surface area nanoparticle catalysts including zeolite encapsulated transition metals and copper/zinc oxide catalysts for hydrogenation and dehydration (potentially replacing precious Pt catalysts in fuel cell applications). A Professor of Chemistry stated the petitioner is a recognized expert in nanoporous and nanoparticulate materials. A Professor of Chemical Engineering stated the petitioner's work is internationally acclaimed and forms the basis for new products in the chemical and petroleum industries. An Associate Professor of Geochemistry addressed the petitioner's silicas' stability and biocompatibility. The petitioner also submitted citation indices showing his published work has been cited more than one hundred times.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that initial letters from the petitioner's close colleagues were not first-hand evidence of acclaim outside his affiliated institution. However, on appeal the petitioner submitted letters from prominent witnesses outside his circle of acquaintances. The numerous independent citations showed that other researchers have been influenced by the petitioner's work and are familiar with it, bolstering the witnesses' observations that his findings are of major significance to the chemistry field. The AAO concluded the record adequately demonstrated that the petitioner's contributions are important not only to the research institutions where he has worked but throughout the greater field, and found the evidence adequate to satisfy this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's authorship of articles appearing in publications such as Journal of Physical Chemistry B, Journal of Materials Chemistry, The Journal of Physical Chemistry, Inorganic Chemistry, and Journal of Chemical Physics. The petitioner also submitted citation indices showing his published articles have been widely cited by numerous independent researchers.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that when judging the influence and impact of published work, the very act of publication is not as reliable a gauge as the citation history. Publication alone may serve as evidence of originality, but it is difficult to conclude that a published article is important or influential without evidence that other researchers relied on the findings. In this case, the significant number of citations demonstrated widespread interest in and reliance on the petitioner's work, showing that many other researchers acknowledged the petitioner's influence and found his work significant. The AAO found the evidence satisfied this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO found that the petitioner satisfied only two of the regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) (original contributions and scholarly articles), falling short of the required minimum of three. The AAO concluded that review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself as a scientist to such an extent that he could be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. The burden of proof remained entirely with the petitioner, who failed to sustain that burden, so the appeal was dismissed.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted a procedural issue regarding evidence submitted on appeal: two e-mails from September and October 2002 reflecting an additional manuscript review came into existence subsequent to the petition's filing date of July 22, 2002, and could not retroactively establish eligibility as of the filing date per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. Additionally, an undated 'Dear Colleague' letter from a Program Officer did not specifically identify the petitioner.
